Name: rate_distortion_model_compression
Title: Rate-Distortion Trade-offs in Model Compression for Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by framing model compression as a rate-distortion problem, we can derive tighter bounds on the trade-off between compression rate and task performance, leading to more efficient and effective compression algorithms.
Related Work: Previous work has explored the connection between rate-distortion theory and model compression, primarily focusing on pruning and quantization techniques. Isik et al. (2021) derived a rate-distortion framework for pruning, while Bu et al. (2019) and Bu et al. (2021) studied the population risk improvement with model compression. However, these works often focus on specific compression methods or empirical evaluations. Our proposal aims to provide a more general and theoretically grounded approach to model compression by deriving tighter rate-distortion bounds and exploring novel compression algorithms.
Abstract: We propose an information-theoretic framework for model compression in neural networks, framed as a rate-distortion problem. By quantifying the trade-off between compression rate and task performance, we aim to derive tighter bounds on the achievable compression efficiency. Our approach involves developing new estimators for task-relevant information and distortion, and using these to guide compression algorithms. We hypothesize that this framework will lead to more efficient and effective compression methods, potentially improving population risk. We validate our approach through experiments on common neural network architectures and datasets, demonstrating the practical benefits of our theoretical insights.
Experiments: Develop an estimator for task-relevant information (e.g., mutual information between the compressed model and the task output).
Derive a rate-distortion bound for a given neural network architecture and dataset, using the information estimator.
Implement a compression algorithm that optimizes the rate-distortion trade-off, such as successive pruning or quantization.
Evaluate the performance of the compressed models on standard benchmarks (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet) using metrics such as accuracy, compression rate, and distortion.
Compare the results with existing compression methods (e.g., L1 pruning, weight quantization) to demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The development of accurate and efficient information estimators is challenging and may introduce biases.
The rate-distortion framework assumes a specific distortion metric, which may not always align with practical performance metrics.
The computational complexity of optimizing the rate-distortion trade-off may be high, especially for large models and datasets.
The theoretical bounds derived may not always be tight, limiting the practical applicability of the results.
